parsing_res_list.block_content: Tonew restaurants to try in the new year
MICHAEL DISKIN
Desnuda Cocina & Bar (above) and Russ & Mimi's (right) are among the many exciting restaurants arriving on the scene.
JONATHAN WIGGS/GLOBE STAFF
RESTAURANTS Continued from Page G1

ork belly bao bunsnd tcos, m ggplant, and grilled steak with chimihurri sauce. Chef Jesus Preciado's nenu draws from Asia and atin Amerca (including his native Colombia). On he cocktai i, nd riks ike et the Heat (made with spicy pisco and beet yrup) and Amarillo Passion (in which equilaesuu quur ssionruit
647Tremott.outh Edon,17-286-2333, www.desnudacocia.com. Smaller plates $6-$18, larger lates $18-$32, cocktails $14-$17.
aia

The latest from Xenia Greek Hospiality, which brought us Bar Vlaha, ra, and more. This time, culinary direcr Brendan Plley and team whisk dinrs to the egean (with a South End ind-set) ia dishes like a ream ruo with ermd sa ro ly ats with ;lack trufle spanakopita; and grilled hole fish. The wine list is devoted sole to selections from the Aegean and astern Mediterranean islands.
370 Harrison Ave., South End, Bosn, 617-514-0700, www.kaiasouthad.com. Dishes $16-$44.
lasonsSteakhouse

Here's what happens when a group f powerhouse South Shore culinary  incluin im ng   uj roup and Kevin Long of Tosca) join rces. This swank Quincy steakhouse is cated in a former Masonic temple and rves up lobster bisque, oysters Rockeller, pappardelle bolognese, salmon ith spiced cauliflower – and, of use, i s.T ful, creative desserts are from pastry  nd M r rt naez, who is bhind  uiny's Niveaux atisserie. in a landcae (incsiy)ocked with steakhouses, it is nice to e a new one open with local ownerp.
1170 Hancock St., Quincy, 617-689818,wrwrw.masonssteakhouse.com. Apetizers $14-$32, entrees $29-$45,eaks $52-$160, sides $6-$14, desserts 12-$19.
cGonagle's Pub
Ea inging excellent fish and chips and sh hospilit t dn Boston,will liy l e me at Mnagle's in Dorchester.is in a blanket ade with sausage from Adams Village

Butcher Shop, smoky seafood chowder with Irish soda bread, shepherd's pie,bangers and mash, and other pub favorites are here, along with wood-fired pizza, brunch (yes, full Irish breakfast),and festive cocktails. Or just drop in for live music and a proper pint of Guinness
]367 Neponset Ave., Dorchester, 617514-4689, wrww.mcgonagles.com. Appetizers $8-$16, pizza $18-$19, entrees $18-$34.
Mikiva Wagvu Shabu House

E If you're starving, cold, and enjoy wagyu beef, Mikiya is calling you. This Chinatown restaurant serves all-youcan-eat wagyu hot pot at a variety of price points; you have 90 minutes to cook a variety of cuts in the broths of your choosing, while eating specialty dishes such as sweet shrimp sashimi and wagyu bone marrow, plus fruit and ice cream for dessert. Mikiya is a project from Chubby Group, whose Kevin Liu is also behind Matsunori Handroll Bar.
Wagyu sets $45-$98 (less with annual membership; free or half-price for kids).
Russ & Mimi's

Named for the parents of owner Kelly Walsh (Sweet Cheeks, Tiger Mama),this Roslindale market offers wine (it's in the former Solera space), olive oil, hot sauce, and other must-haves. But it's the food that has the neighborhood most

excited. Start the morning with egg wraps, overnight oats, cheesy grits, and cumin-spiced black beans with fried egg, salsa, and avocado; then segue into the"Cubano, veggie grinder, muff uletta,charred broccoli Caesar" part of the day.Chef Stephen Marcaurelle (the Nautilus Pier 4, Tres Gatos) helped design the menu.
司16 Birch St., Roslindale, 617-8336950, wrww.russandmimis.com. Dishes
Soul on Shawmut

Roxbury-raised chef Aadam Abdul Musawwir began serving prepared meals and pop-up dinners as Aadam's Halal Meals. Now, he and two siblings have pd ul n mt o d their community. Lightly updated sou food classics are on the menu, along with Buffalo chicken egg rolls, grilled cheese burgers, and more. Fried chick en with Old Bay honey, smoked mad and cheese, slow-cooked collard greens with sauteed cabbage, sweet potato  cheesecake, and hibiscus watermelon  juice sounds pretty unbeatable for din ner tonight.
E 722A Shawmut Ave., Roxbury, 617238-7525, www.soulonshawmut.com.Sandwiches $10-$18,fried chicken $7-$18, sides $5, desserts $3-$6.
Standard Italian

Where others fail, there is Italian food. Standard Italian, in the Eastern Standard complex looming over Fenway, replaces the former All That Fish +Oyster, and it's a smart move. Lasagna bologneeporcha,andpreso martinis could just get us all through the winter. Culinary director Brian Rae is in his element, having previously worked at Coppa, Faccia a Faccia, and Rialto.
司771 Beacon St., Fenway, Boston, 857305-3095,www.standarditalian.com Appetizers $5-$20, entrees $28-$42, des  serts $9-$12.
Zurito

It's named for the small glass of beer that often accompanies pintxos, the Basque take on tapas, and this Beacon Hill restaurant serves up plenty: Gildas skewers of anchovies, peppers, and ol ives; griddled shrimp with garlic and chile oil; mini croissants stuffed with ja mon iberico; squid ink risotto. Cocktails such as the sherry-based Perfect Bam boo and fizzy glasses of tzakolina are  perfect accompaniments. Zurito, locat ed in the longtime Bin 26 space, is rur by chef Jamie Bissonnette, Babak Bina and Andy Cartin of BCB3 Hospitality, al so behind Somaek, Temple Records and Sushi @ Temple Records.
司a 857-305-3177,www.zuritoboston.com Pintxos $4.50-$12, raciones $9-$19 platos especiales $17-$125.
Devra First can be reached at devra.first@globe.com. Follow her @deurafirst.
Restaurants continue to innovate in mocktail realm
MOCKTAILS

添司添ons that can really make a nonlcoholic drink look and taste early identical to an alcoholic ne," ays ol tlas a mixologist t the Avery Bar at the Rit-CarlnBon."The boom f ons has really picked up in the 中
"[Mocktails] are certainly e truthof t all s that the nonlcoholic or low-ABV consumer s sustaining through much of n ear," ys Nick Mo nral manager of Banners Kitch  .
]mited to just a soda water and ranberry, or a sugar bomb of ices made with whatever was ing around behind the ar.onsumers wanted and de   "
]f – here are 12 mocktail destiations to try every month of the ear.
he & Louie's

be&oo $14 mocktail menu. Their New a Nw itz mies erroof sparkling Cava with nonlcoholic tequila, cucumr,me, and mint for a spa-like our.Good for: expense-account ining.793 Boylston S, Boston,17-536-6300
lcove

oO In the West End, cozy Alcove oes an $11 mocktail menu with nusual offerings like Papi's unch, with grilled pineapple yrup and Athletic Free Wave onalcoholic IPA, or a Queen's ee, made with the herbaceous onalcoholic spirit Garden 108nd honey. Good for: T.D. Garen pre-game hangouts. 50 Loveoy Wharf, Boston, 617-248-0050
AveryBarattheitz-Cao Boston
This might be one of Boston's nost classic of cocktail refuges,put it also caters to modern mo
ioned employs decaf English breakfast tea; a Cucumber Gimless is an aromatic booze-free  gin-and-cucumber palate cleanser. Good for: sophisticated postwork drinks. 10 Avery St., Boston, 617-574-7176
Baleia

EM This stylish new Portuguese restaurant in the South End,from the team behind Gufo and SRV, welcomes a cosmopolitan crowd eager to experiment with nonalcoholic delights. Try the Tropical Amargo, an aperitif made with coconut-chai, orange,lime, pineapple, and cofee, plus spirit-free Campari. Good for:date night. 264 East Berkeley St.,Boston, 617-505-3243
Bistro du Midi
Above: Warming mocktails  at Capo in South Boston.Left: A cosmopolitan at  Bistro du Midi.

Public Garden, and it serves far  more than wine. A $10 mocktail  menu has spins on classics like tiki punch, a cosmopolitan, and a spicy citrus mule. Good for: dinner with the parents. 272 Boylston St., Boston, 617-279-8000
Capo

司This South Boston red-sauce lair might seem like the type of spot to throw back icy martinis (they do host Sinatra Sundays, after all) paired with big plates of rigatoni Bolognese, but they also have warming $9 mocktails, like the Far From the Tree, with toasty apple cider, cardamom,allspice, thyme, and egg whites.In addition to catering to nondrinkers, they accommodate a range of palates, with gluten-free pasta on request. Good for: a night out with a group. 443 West Broadway, Boston, 617-993-8080
Hunter's

This is your spot for biscuits,fried chicken, mac-and-cheese …in other words, resolutionbreaking meals. That said: This dark, cozy bar and kitchen also pays attention to mocktails,which are a relative steal at $6.
bacon-topped cornbread with refreshing house-made watermelon juice and soda. Good for:comfort food. 110 Dorchester St,Boston, 617-221-4200
La Padrona

Stylish, swanky, and with a stellar chef attached (Jody Adams), this Italian destination inside Raffles Boston is the ultimate in special-occasion dining:The silken tagliatelle alone is worth the trip up that grand staircase. Sure, there's also an extensive martini menu, but spiritfree drinks also get proper due.A nonalcoholic tangerine marga rita with spirit-free tequila and lime is worth toasting. Not quite ready to abstain? There's also an extensive low-ABV list featuring treats like a Lambrusco Cobbler,made with black currant and berries. Good for: a celebratory splurge. 38 Trinity Place, Boston,617-221-4200
Midnight Morning

This cheery Roslindale bistro specializes in comfort food: sausage dip, mushroom-and-creamcheese wontons, burgers, and fried pickles. It makes nondrinkers feel comfy, too, with a nice $12 selection of zero-proof drinks: The Regret Nothing,with nonalcoholic Ritual Proof tequila, jalapeno, and lime, is true to its name. Good for: a laidback weeknight dinner. 14 Birch St., Roslindale, 617-390-5830
Trina's Starlite Lounge

Ta This industry hangout takes dive-bar food to another level —they pioneered dressed-up tater tots and mac-and-cheese before it was fashionable. Here, $5 cans of Miller High Life share real estate with cheeky mocktails like the Phony Negroni, a nonalcoholic version of the classic, and Island Life, Baby, made with a booze-free, floral Fluère Botanic Blend. Good for: dive-bar-dining without the buzz. 3 Beacon St.,Somerville, 617-576-0006
Tuscan Brands

serves reliable food in convenient locations and a thoughtfully curated list of spirit-free sips,like a Tea Party with coconut water, chai tea, and brown sugar,and the Cos-No-Politan, made with honey water, cranberry,rooibos tea, and lime. Beverage director Jose Luis Betancur also has a fondness for tart Italian sodas like Chinotto or Stappi Gassosa. Good for: pleasing a crowd (or kids). 64 Seaport Boulevard,Boston, 617-303-7300, and other locations
Woods Hill Pier 4
Owner Kristin Canty is known for serving pasture
Kara Baskin can be reached at kara.baskin@globe.com. Follow her @kcbaskin.

raised meat and other fresh healthy ingredients from her Farm at Woods Hill in New Hampshire at this waterfront branch of her West Concord original. Mocktails (most are $8and up) also showcase ingredi ents and garnishes from her farm. Sip a Fly-Tai, made with pineapple, orange, lime, almonc orgeat, and ginger ale. Good for entertaining out-of-towners.00Pier 4 Blud., Boston, 617-9814577
TicketTo  The Arts
BOSTON CHAMBER MUSIC SOCIETY

DIRECTED BY TASIA A. JONES Set in 1950's Brooklyn,two sisters mourn the loss of theirmother with Hollywoodfims,daydreams,and lots of cookies.When a shocking decisionismadebthiraherthefamilmt find new meaning in what makes a home. Tickets at lyricstage.com or by calling the box office at
617-585-5678MUSIC Symphony PRO Mark Churchill, Music Diretor
SPM CELEBRATES THEA MUSGRAVE
Musgraves Turbulent Landscapes,Elgar Enigma Variations, Wm.Walton PortsmouthPoit. at Jan257:30pHudsoHhcoolSJ 3:00pmSt.John'sFounders allShew Tix: https://www.symphonypromusica.org

RUSSIAN WINTER Sun. 1/12 at 3PM, Sanders Theatre Prokofiev: Quintet in G minor, Op.39Arensky: Piano Trio No.1in D minorO.32Glazunov:nQuntinorO.9$30-$68; Sr: $4off;Student:617.349.0086/bostonchambermusic.org
MARK MORRISDANCEGROUP
BACK IN BOSTON FOR THE 1ST TIME IN 6 YEARS!

Legendary dance company Mark Morris Dance Group performs "The Look of Love"—a mustsee homaer-ppurt Bacharach,including"SayaLittle Prayer,""What the World Needs Now" & more. A powerf fusion of dance andmusic feat.a live band and vocals sung by Broadway star Marcy Harriell,"is song, says more than just words could ever
